exemplar.title: How did netflix's password-sharing restrictions (2023) influenced its brand perception and financial performance?

detailedExemplarData.title: How did netflix's password-sharing restrictions (2023) influenced its brand perception and financial performance?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clearly recognises and defines the key concept of change
Consistently links findings (e.g., profit margins, consumer backlash) back to change
Good analytical thinking in tying metrics to organizational shifts
Strong focus on Netflix context across sections
weaknesses: Limited engagement with established change-management frameworks (e.g., Lewinâs model)
Concept often inserted rather than deeply integrated into causal analysis
SWOT insights are not revisited or applied in later sections
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments point to a strong understanding of the key concept and clear analytical thinking tied to Netflix, matching the band-4 descriptor. However, the analysis slips into naming âchangeâ without embedding theoretical frameworks, preventing full integration required for the top band.

strengths: Includes four supporting documents, meeting the 3â5 requirement
Documents are relevant and provide both quantitative and qualitative evidence
Depth is adequate, with multi-quarter figures and consumer quotes
weaknesses: Limited breadth of perspectives (mostly English-language media)
No critical evaluation of source reliability or bias
Supporting figures are not highlighted for reader guidance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The set of documents is relevant and sufficiently in-depth, fitting the band-3 descriptor. A broader range of sources and critical appraisal would be needed for the top band.

strengths: Selection of four distinct tools (SWOT, Ansoff, 4 Ps, ratios) covers strategic, marketing and financial lenses
Tools chosen are relevant to the research question
Demonstrates awareness of both qualitative and quantitative analysis
weaknesses: Applications of some tools are descriptive rather than incisive
No rationale provided for choosing each tool
Certain analyses (e.g., SWOT, Ansoff) lack depth and external benchmarking
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the selection of tools is effective, their application is only adequately applied, aligning with the band-3 descriptor. Deeper justification and more incisive use of certain models would move this to band 4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Uses multi-period net-income and revenue comparisons effectively
Links consumer backlash to competitive threats in the analysis
Goes beyond description with evaluation of post-change impacts
Integrates quantitative data with qualitative insights
weaknesses: Data scope is limited (no brand-perception metrics or industry benchmarks)
Integration between tools is sometimes superficial
Formatting errors (e.g., misformatted numbers) risk reader confusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis is mostly effective with some integration across tools and data, meeting the band-4 descriptor. To reach the top band, the work would need wider data sources and deeper triangulation between models.

strengths: Conclusions are consistent with the presented evidence
Explicitly answers the research question about profitability impacts
weaknesses: Conclusions are too brief and focus narrowly on profitability
Lack of discussion on brand-perception findings and research limitations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusions fully consistent with evidence and directly answer the research question, satisfying the band-3 descriptor. A more comprehensive wrap-up of findings and limitations would strengthen this section.

strengths: Logical organization with clear table of contents and sectional headings
Consistent flow from introduction through to conclusion
weaknesses: Table of contents lacks clear hierarchy (indenting of subsections)
Occasional sections do not explicitly link back to the research question
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary demonstrates appropriate structure throughout, meeting the band-2 descriptor. Minor refinements in navigation and explicit RQ linking would enhance readability.

strengths: All required IB elements are present (title page, word count, declaration, RQ, key concept, references, appendices)
Formatting generally meets IB expectations
weaknesses: Declaration formatting is cluttered and inconsistent
Citation style in references lacks standardization (missing access dates)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All required elements are included, fulfilling the band-2 descriptor. Cleaning up formatting and standardizing citations would polish the presentation further.

strandSelections.reasoning: The commentary is logically organised with a clear table of contents, sectional headings and consistent flow from introduction to conclusion, meeting expectations for appropriate structure throughout.

The financial statement and news articles are clearly pertinent to the research question and supply both quantitative and qualitative evidence. Depth is adequate (e.g., multi-quarter profit figures, direct consumer quotes) but the set is narrowâlargely English-language media and one primary financial tableâwith limited breadth of perspectives, so it fits the ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ descriptor rather than the ârange of ideasâ demanded for top band.

All IB-required elementsâtitle page with word count, declaration, RQ, key concept, references and appendicesâare present. While formatting is occasionally messy, nothing appears missing, fulfilling the highest presentation band.
Four supporting documents (financial statement extract and three recent media articles) are attached, placing the IA within the 3â5 range specified for this band.

Each tool is applied to Netflixâs situation with some specificity (e.g., linking $7.99 add-on fee to âPriceâ and to margins). However, application is often descriptive and occasionally superficialâSWOT points are not quantified, and Ansoff discussion lacks rival comparisonâso relevance is clear but not consistently incisive, matching the "adequately applied" level.

The IA uses multi-period net-income and revenue figures and cites subscriber data, representing an adequate, mostly accurate selection of data. Yet data are limited to profitability; no brand-perception metrics or industry benchmarks are incorporated, preventing classification as âsufficientâ or âeffectiveâ.

Analysis moves beyond mere descriptionâfor instance, comparing average margins pre- and post-change and relating consumer backlash to competitive threats. Still, evaluation is brief, integration between tools is limited, and counter-arguments are thin, so the work meets the âsome analysisâ standard rather than âmostly effectiveâ.

The final paragraph explicitly answers the research question, stating the policy raised profitability but leaves long-term brand effects uncertain. Although terse, it constitutes a direct response, satisfying the top-band requirement for this strand.

The student selects four distinct and appropriate tools (SWOT, Ansoff, 4 Ps, profitability ratios), each suited to examining strategic, marketing and financial aspects of the question. The combination demonstrates awareness of both qualitative and quantitative lenses, satisfying the rubricâs expectation of an effective selection.

Conclusions on increased profitability align with the ratio calculations presented, but the claim that brand perception âsuccessfully addressed market problemsâ is insufficiently evidenced. Thus, only some conclusions are firmly rooted in the data supplied.

All sections keep the focus firmly on Netflix. Financial ratios, subscriber numbers and consumer sentiment are consistently tied back to the firmâs contemporary context. Links are explicitly made between the policy and brand perception / profitability across multiple tools. Nevertheless, some analysis remains siloed (e.g., SWOT points not revisited later), preventing full, seamless integration; hence a strong but not outstanding performance.

The student clearly recognises that the investigation is framed by the key concept of change, offers a textbook definition, and repeatedly links individual findings (e.g., increase in net-profit margin, customer backlash) to that concept.  However, the discussion often slips into simply inserting the word âchangeâ rather than weaving the concept through deeper causal analysis (for example, no exploration of incremental vs. disruptive change or of change-management models).  Integration is therefore evident but partial, aligning best with the descriptor of "good understanding with clear analytical thinking" rather than the fully integrated analytical approach required for the top band.

annotations.comment: The research questionâs phrasing (âinfluencedâ) is grammatically inconsistent; consider rephrasing to âHow did Netflixâs password-sharing restrictions (2023) influenceâ¦?â for clarity. (Suggestion)

The key concept âChangeâ is named but lacks engagement with established change-management frameworks (e.g., Lewinâs model or incremental vs disruptive change). Embed theoretical depth. (Weakness)
All required IB elements are present, but formatting of the declaration is cluttered. Clean up spacing and font consistency. (Suggestion)
The table of contents is comprehensive but lacks clear hierarchy; indent subsections consistently to improve navigability. (Suggestion)
Introduction lists tools but provides no rationale for their selection; briefly justify why each tool suits the research question. (Question)
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: SWOT section repeats the word âchangeâ rather than applying SWOT insights to specific organizational outcomes, reducing analytical depth. (Weakness)
Strengths paragraph cites the $7.99 fee but does not quantify its impact on subscriber numbers. Include numerical evidence from supporting docs. (Suggestion)
Weaknesses discussion mentions privacy concerns but does not reference survey or sentiment data. Integrate specific metrics or quotes. (Suggestion)
The opportunities paragraph suggests personalized pricing but lacks competitor benchmarks. Compare with market rates at Disney+ or HBO. (Suggestion)
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Threats paragraph summarizes SWOT but misses linking threats back to change-management strategy. Tie threats to risk-mitigation frameworks. (Suggestion)
Ansoff Matrix lacks reference to industry growth rates; include market data to validate penetration potential. (Weakness)
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Four Ps âProductâ repeats descriptive change narrative without linking to brand-equity theory. Discuss product changes in branding frameworks. (Suggestion)
Price analysis describes reactions but omits quantitative subscriber churn or downgrades; incorporate data to support claims. (Suggestion)
Net profit margin formula is correct but the revenue denominator is misformatted (8.641.253 instead of 8,641,253), risking misinterpretation. (Weakness)
Pre-change average net profit margin is calculated but without noting outlier effects; discuss variability impact on comparability. (Suggestion)
Post-change ratio comparison omits explicit mention of research question; reiterate how ratios answer the question. (Suggestion)
Conclusion is too brief and focuses solely on profitability; expand to address brand-perception findings and research limitations. (Weakness)
Final section reiterates metrics without synthesizing tool insights; weave in SWOT and Ansoff conclusions to enhance integration. (Suggestion)
References list is comprehensive but inconsistent (some URLs lack access dates); standardize citation format. (Suggestion)
Appendix contains extensive data tables but lacks highlighting of directly relevant figures; mark key entries to guide readers. (Suggestion)
Supporting Document 2 is cited but not critically evaluated for bias or source reliability; briefly assess its credibility. (Suggestion)
Social media quotes illustrate backlash but lack triangulation with sentiment indices; integrate survey data or social-listening metrics. (Suggestion)
Supporting Document 3 provides anecdotal cases but no demographic context; include participant profiles or representativeness. (Question)
Supporting Document 4 discussion of Antenna growth data is not incorporated into main analysis; explicitly reference growth surge in evaluation. (Suggestion)
